1: ὁ κληρονόμος νήπιός ἐστιν (the heir is a minor/underage child, G3516) — nēpios denotes the child not yet of legal age, who possesses the inheritance legally but cannot access it independently. The analogy maps onto the OT period: Israel was heir to the promise but under guardianship, not yet in full possession.
2: ὑπὸ ἐπιτρόπους ἐστὶν καὶ οἰκονόμους (under guardians and trustees) — two classes of people managing the minor's affairs: epitropoi (guardians of the person) and oikonomoi (trustees of the estate). The law performed both functions: guarding conduct and managing the promise-inheritance before the fullness of time.
3: ὑπὸ τὰ στοιχεῖα τοῦ κόσμου (under the elemental spirits/principles of the world, G4747) — stoicheia is debated: elemental principles of learning (ABCs), elemental heavenly bodies, or elemental spiritual powers. The context (enslaved condition requiring divine redemption) suggests more than mere religious categories; the same term appears at v.9 for the powers the Galatians would return to.
4: ὅτε δὲ ἦλθεν τὸ πλήρωμα τοῦ χρόνου (but when the fullness of time came, G4138) — plērōma tou chronou: not merely calendar timing but the completion/filling-up of the appointed period. History was moving toward this moment. ἐξαπέστειλεν ὁ θεὸς τὸν υἱὸν αὐτοῦ (God sent out his Son, G1821) — the compound exapostellō implies sending out from the divine sphere, presupposing the Son's pre-existence. γενόμενον ἐκ γυναικός, γενόμενον ὑπὸ νόμον (born of a woman, born under the law) — the two aorist participles mark the double contingency of the Incarnation: entering human nature and entering the specific covenant domain of the law.
5: ἵνα τοὺς ὑπὸ νόμον ἐξαγοράσῃ (to redeem those under the law, G1805) — the aorist subjunctive of purpose; exagorazō (to buy out of the marketplace) indicates payment and liberation from a condition of bondage. ἵνα τὴν υἱοθεσίαν ἀπολάβωμεν (that we might receive adoption to sonship, G5206) — apolabōmen (receive back/in full) suggests receiving what was always promised but not yet possessed. Huiothesia is the formal legal adoption-term, as in Rom 8:15,23.
6: ἐξαπέστειλεν ὁ θεὸς τὸ πνεῦμα τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ (God sent out the Spirit of his Son) — the compound exapostellō is the same verb as v.4 for sending the Son; the Spirit's sending parallels the Son's sending. τοῦ υἱοῦ αὐτοῦ (of his Son) — the Spirit is identified as the Son's own Spirit, grounding the believer's filial cry in the Son's own relationship with the Father. κρᾶζον Ἀββά, ὁ πατήρ (crying Abba, Father, G2896) — the present participle describes ongoing crying; the Spirit's cry is continuous, not a one-time event.
7: ὥστε οὐκέτι εἶ δοῦλος ἀλλὰ υἱός (so that you are no longer a slave but a son) — the transition from slave-status to son-status is the direct consequence of the double sending (Son and Spirit) of vv.4-6. The address shifts to second singular: this is personal and specific, not merely corporate. κληρονόμος διὰ θεοῦ (heir through God) — the inheritance is now accessible through divine action rather than held in suspension under guardianship.
8: τοῖς φύσει μὴ οὖσιν θεοῖς ἐδουλεύσατε (you were slaves to those who by nature are not gods) — physis (G5449) marks the ontological claim: the pagan deities have no real divine nature. The enslavement was real but directed at non-entities; the more striking slavery would be returning to bondage of any kind after being freed (v.9).
9: νῦν δὲ γνόντες θεόν, μᾶλλον δὲ γνωσθέντες ὑπὸ θεοῦ (but now knowing God, or rather being known by God) — the correction (mallon de = but rather) is theologically significant: Paul prefers the passive construction to the active, since divine initiative precedes and grounds human knowing. The knowing is relational, not merely cognitive. πῶς ἐπιστρέφετε πάλιν (how are you turning back again?) — the present tense captures the in-process reversal; there is still time.
10: ἡμέρας παρατηρεῖσθε καὶ μῆνας καὶ καιροὺς καὶ ἐνιαυτούς (you observe days and months and seasons and years, G3906) — the calendar observances are symptoms rather than the disease; the disease is re-submission to law as a means of standing before God. The list moves from smallest to largest temporal unit, suggesting comprehensive calendar-compliance.
11: φοβοῦμαι ὑμᾶς (I fear for you) — the first person present expression of pastoral anxiety; Paul's fear is not about them as persons but about the possible futility of his previous labor among them. μή πως εἰκῇ κεκοπίακα εἰς ὑμᾶς (lest somehow I have labored in vain among you) — the perfect kekopika (I have labored) marks the sustained investment; the adverb eikē (in vain, without result) is the feared outcome.
12: γίνεσθε ὡς ἐγώ, ὅτι κἀγὼ ὡς ὑμεῖς (become like me, because I also became like you) — the reciprocal becoming: Paul became like Gentiles (freed from the law's jurisdiction) in his apostolic practice (cf. 1 Cor 9:21); now he calls them to become like him (standing in the freedom of Christ). The ethical argument is autobiographical before it is propositional.
13: δι᾽ ἀσθένειαν τῆς σαρκός (because of weakness of the flesh, G4561 disp=4) — here sarx is neutral: physical flesh, bodily weakness. The illness is not named but was Paul's occasion for first preaching in Galatia; the providential use of personal weakness is an implicit argument against flesh-reliance.
14: τὸν πειρασμὸν ὑμῶν ἐν τῇ σαρκί μου (your trial in my flesh) — Paul acknowledges that his bodily condition was itself a test for the Galatians: would they reject someone physically afflicted? ὡς ἄγγελον θεοῦ ἐδέξασθέ με, ὡς Χριστὸν Ἰησοῦν (you received me as an angel of God, as Christ Jesus) — the reception was apostolic: through Paul's preaching they encountered Christ himself (cf. 4:19).
15: ὁ μακαρισμὸς ὑμῶν (your blessing/congratulation of me) — the term makarismos (G3108) refers to the previous beatitude/blessing the Galatians pronounced over Paul; the question "where is it now?" implies its disappearance. The hypothetical self-sacrifice ("you would have plucked out your eyes") suggests a particularly deep affection and commitment that has since cooled.
16: ἐχθρὸς ὑμῶν γέγονα ἀληθεύων ὑμῖν (have I become your enemy by speaking truth to you?) — the perfect gegona (I have become) asks whether a change has occurred in their perception of him; truth-speaking is the thing that produces enmity when the audience doesn't want to hear it.
17: ζηλοῦσιν ὑμᾶς οὐ καλῶς (they are zealous for you, but not in a good way) — the present tense of the agitators' ongoing activity; zēloō (G2206) is the verb for zealous pursuit; the "not well" qualifies the motive. ἐκκλεῖσαι ὑμᾶς θέλουσιν (they want to shut you out) — ekkleiō (G1576) is to shut out, exclude; the agitators want to cut the Galatians off from Paul's gospel-network so they become dependent on the agitators' alternative.
18: καλὸν δὲ ζηλοῦσθαι ἐν καλῷ πάντοτε (it is good to be zealously sought always in what is good) — Paul acknowledges that zeal-toward-someone is not inherently bad; the problem is the purpose and quality of the zeal. The "not only when I am present" (en tō pareinai me) refers to Paul's own presence: during his presence the Galatians' zeal was rightly directed.
19: τεκνία μου, οὓς πάλιν ὠδίνω (my little children, for whom I am again in labor pains, G5605) — the birth-pangs metaphor (ōdinō is the verb for labor-pains in childbirth) applied to apostolic travail; Paul is experiencing the same intensity as the initial evangelism. μέχρις οὗ μορφωθῇ Χριστὸς ἐν ὑμῖν (until Christ is formed in you, G3445) — the aorist passive subjunctive of morphoō: Christ taking shape/form within the community is the goal of the apostolic labor. The morphē language is the same root as Phil 2:6-7 (morphē theou/morphēn doulou).
20: ἠθελον δὲ παρεῖναι πρὸς ὑμᾶς ἄρτι (I wish I could be with you now) — the imperfect ēthelon indicates an unfulfilled desire in the present: he wants to be there but cannot. ἀπορούμαι ἐν ὑμῖν (I am at a loss about you, G639) — the present passive: Paul is in a state of perplexity about them, uncertain what to think. The honesty is pastoral rather than rhetorical.
21: λέγετέ μοι, οἱ ὑπὸ νόμον θέλοντες εἶναι (tell me, you who want to be under law) — the address is pointed: oî hypo nomon thelontes einai (those desiring to be under the law). The thelontes (wanting, present active participle) implies active desire, not passive entrapment. Paul then asks whether they know what the law says — a rhetorical challenge to attend to what the law itself teaches about being under it.
22: γέγραπται γὰρ ὅτι Ἀβραὰμ δύο υἱοὺς ἔσχεν (for it is written that Abraham had two sons) — the perfect gegraptai (it stands written) is the standard citation formula. The two sons structure the entire subsequent allegory: Ishmael from the slave woman (Hagar) by natural means, Isaac from the free woman (Sarah) by promise.
23: ὁ μὲν ἐκ τῆς παιδίσκης κατὰ σάρκα γεγέννηται (the one from the slave woman was born according to the flesh, G4561 disp=4) — kata sarka here means by natural biological means, without divine promise-involvement. ὁ δὲ ἐκ τῆς ἐλευθέρας δι᾽ ἐπαγγελίας (but the one from the free woman through promise) — the preposition dia specifies the mechanism: the promise was the means by which Isaac came into being.
24: ἅτινά ἐστιν ἀλληγορούμενα (which things are being allegorized/taken figuratively, G238) — the present passive participle: Paul is now reading the narrative figuratively, not claiming that the narrative is non-historical. αὗται γάρ εἰσιν δύο διαθῆκαι (for these women represent two covenants) — the two women are not merely private individuals but typological representatives of two covenant-administrations: Sinai (Hagar) and the promise (Sarah).
25: τὸ δὲ Ἅγαρ Σινᾶ ὄρος ἐστὶν ἐν τῇ Ἀραβίᾳ (now Hagar is Mount Sinai in Arabia) — the identification is geographical and typological: Hagar's territory is Arabia, where Sinai is located; the Sinai covenant belongs in the same symbolic geography as Hagar's bondage. συστοιχεῖ δὲ τῇ νῦν Ἱερουσαλήμ (she corresponds to the present Jerusalem, G4960) — the present Jerusalem (under Rome, under the law's continued claim) is in the Hagar-line, not the Sarah-line.
26: ἡ δὲ ἄνω Ἱερουσαλήμ (but the Jerusalem above) — the heavenly Jerusalem (cf. Heb 12:22; Rev 21:2) is in the Sarah-line: free. ἥτις ἐστὶν μήτηρ ἡμῶν (who is our mother) — the collective identification: believers are children of the free Jerusalem, not the enslaved one. The mother-city metaphor places their identity in the eschatological rather than the earthly Jerusalem.
27: The citation from Isa 54:1 (LXX) — εὐφράνθητι, στεῖρα ἡ οὐ τίκτουσα (rejoice, barren woman who does not bear) — describes the restoration of Zion after exile; Paul applies it to Sarah and to the Jerusalem-above: the woman who seemed barren (Sarah; the new covenant community) will produce more children than the apparently fruitful woman (Hagar; law-covenant Israel). The oti (because) introduces the reason: the barren woman will have more children.
28: ἡμεῖς δέ, ἀδελφοί, κατὰ Ἰσαὰκ ἐπαγγελίας τέκνα ἐσμέν (now we, brothers, like Isaac, are children of promise) — the application to the present community: they are in the Isaac-line, children born of promise rather than of natural process. The identification with Isaac and the free woman is the ground of the freedom that follows in ch.5.
29: ὁ κατὰ σάρκα γεννηθεὶς ἐδίωκεν τὸν κατὰ πνεῦμα (the one born according to the flesh was persecuting the one according to the Spirit) — Paul identifies the Galatians' present situation with the Genesis pattern; the natural-birth line persecutes the promise-birth line. The hostility of circumcision-advocates toward Paul's uncircumcised-Gentile communities is this pattern repeated.
30: The Scripture citation (Gen 21:10) — ἔκβαλε τὴν παιδίσκην καὶ τὸν υἱὸν αὐτῆς (cast out the slave woman and her son) — is Sarah's command to Abraham, recorded in Genesis and treated as scripture's own word. οὐ γὰρ μὴ κληρονομήσῃ ὁ υἱὸς τῆς παιδίσκης μετὰ τοῦ υἱοῦ τῆς ἐλευθέρας (for the son of the slave woman will not inherit with the son of the free woman) — the double negative (ou... mē) is emphatic: absolute exclusion from shared inheritance.
31: διό, ἀδελφοί, οὐκ ἐσμὲν παιδίσκης τέκνα ἀλλὰ τῆς ἐλευθέρας (therefore, brothers, we are not children of the slave woman but of the free woman) — the conclusion drawn from the allegory: the community's identity is in the free woman's line. The absence of the article before eleutherας gives the characteristic a qualitative force: we are children of the one characterized by freedom, not bondage.
